next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

The rail of the observation platform had already burned the heel of Mina's hand by eight-thirty. Sun came off the bay white and flat, then broke pink where it caught the spoonbills lifting over the mangroves. The deck boards still carried yesterday's guano dust in their grooves no matter how hard she had scrubbed at dawn. It only took one wet breeze for the place to smell like hot pine, bird shit, lemon cleaner, and the bait shrimp somebody had forgotten in the ice chest under the stairs.

Beto was on the outside of the rail again, balancing on the beam in his half-size-too-big camp polo while he looped fresh bunting around a post. His sneakers knocked wood in a careless rhythm. One wrist showed the pale crescent burns he had collected on the kitchen flat-top before he had learned to stop grabbing hot pans like they insulted him personally.

"If you fall in front of donors, I will tell them you are an interpretive exhibit," Imogen said.

"Then tell them to tip," Beto said, not climbing back inside until he saw the look on her face and decided he preferred employment.

Imogen had forced her silver hair into a pinned knot that made her look almost ceremonial until the wind lifted one strand loose over her cheek. She wore the camp's faded green field shirt with a clean scarf tucked at the throat, the closest thing Rookery Key had to formal wear. Behind her the main house screens rattled and the generator thrummed through the planks. The camp always sounded temporary on mornings when it most needed to look permanent.

open_hooks_to_carry: The bat route takes Mina and Rhea through an abandoned intake tunnel marked with BrightCurrent signage.
Mina recognizes the annex as part of the biotech network she once worked around.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...leep.

Mina stayed at the table with her ruined appetite and the last bitter inch of coffee. The dawn light had brightened enough to show every scratch in the wood. Outside, a skiff engine started somewhere far off in the channels and flattened into distance. The camp woke in pieces around her: a shower pipe rattling, Beto swearing softly when something metal hit the floor in the kitchen annex, one guest beginning a forced-cheerful conversation with nobody yet visible.

Rhea had meant to warn her. She had done that. Mina understood the geometry now more clearly than she had yesterday: the thin walls, the payroll, the marriage, the fact that Tomas was not some private compromise she could keep sealed away from the rest of the camp. But what stayed in her after the warning was not fear. Not first.

It was the six hours in the mosquitoes. The quiet competence. The story about the crying graduate student. The tobacco tin full of pencil stubs. The way Rhea had seen her nerves and named the better thing underneath them instead.

Mina had spent the last day treating Rhea as a structure to get around. By the time she finally stood and took her plate to the sink, that lie was gone. What replaced it was worse and far less useful. She wanted another night beside Rhea under the mangroves, another hour of clipped instructions and dry jokes and exact hands on wet net line. She wanted the woman's mind, which was the real trouble. She wanted to be looked at that way again and not found sloppy.

With Tomas, wanting had been a bargain and a door. With Rhea it already felt like a measurement.
state_deltas_required: Mina's attraction to Rhea and the shock of finding BrightCurrent infrastructure beneath the camp wetlands
Rhea joins the affair on terms of her own, and Mina realizes the creek is physically connected to her old disgrace.
